behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): this behavior depends on real filesystem watch recursion semantics (root vs nested paths), which are OS/watcher-runtime interactions, not just local function logic. Rationale B (mock risk): injecting synthetic events can miss real non-recursive watcher defaults or platform-specific path emission quirks; a mock can pass while production drops subdirectory files. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text requires actual placement of a file in a subdirectory and observing daemon pickup, which is real FS event behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp directory, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file there, and assert one transformed output appears in the output directory for that nested file path.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): the stated behavior is path+time-window dedup decision logic, which can be exercised at one object/function boundary with controlled clock input. Rationale B (infra need): no real OS/network/subprocess is required to expose wrong equality, TTL comparison, or window-boundary bugs. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text focuses on deduplication logic itself, not watcher/queue/FS handoff.
behaviors.test_description: Call the dedup component directly with the same normalized file path twice under a fake clock and configurable window; assert first request is accepted and second is rejected until the window elapses.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): duplicate-event bursts are produced by real OS/file-watcher behavior and can vary by platform and write pattern. Rationale B (mock risk): synthetic single-event or idealized duplicate-event mocks may not reproduce timing/coalescing races that trigger double-processing in production. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text explicitly ties correctness to operating-system-emitted multiple filesystem events.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon with a real watched directory, perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (e.g., write+rename or chunked append), and assert only one pipeline execution/output is produced for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): directory deletion/recreation while a watcher is active is an OS-level lifecycle event requiring real watcher reattachment behavior. Rationale B (mock risk): mocked watcher callbacks can hide lost watch handles, inode replacement issues, or missed re-subscription after recreation. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text explicitly requires remove+recreate of the real watched directory during runtime.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real temp watched path, remove that directory, recreate it with the same path, add a new file, and assert the daemon processes the new file without daemon restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): simultaneous arrivals combine real FS event ordering with timestamp metadata read from the filesystem; real timing and metadata resolution can affect order correctness. Rationale B (mock risk): in-process fakes usually provide idealized deterministic timestamps/events and can miss production mis-ordering due to coarse timestamp granularity or race timing. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text ties order to actual file arrival timing and filesystem creation timestamps.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on a real watched directory, create several files near-simultaneously (controlled script), capture processing order, and assert it matches the files’ filesystem creation timestamps (with deterministic tie-break rule if equal).

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Rationale A (failure boundary): partial-write handling depends on real file-write progression, event timing, and readiness detection under OS/file semantics. Rationale B (mock risk): mocks often represent writes as atomic, so they can pass while production processes incomplete files during long/chunked writes. Rejection gate: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior text explicitly requires handling a file still being written at event time.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real watched directory, start a slow/chunked writer to a file, verify no processing/output occurs mid-write, then close the writer and assert processing happens once after completion.
